elements.sent-2411-001: Robotic manipulation refers to the autonomous handling and interaction of robots with objects using advanced techniques in robotics and artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-002: The advent of powerful tools such as large language models (LLMs) and large vision-language models (LVLMs) has significantly enhanced the capabilities of these robots in environmental perception and decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, the introduction of these intelligent agents has led to security threats such as jailbreak attacks and adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this research, we take a further step by proposing a backdoor attack specifically targeting robotic manipulation and, for the first time, implementing backdoor attack in the physical world.
elements.sent-2411-005: By embedding a backdoor visual language model into the visual perception module within the robotic system, we successfully mislead the robotic arm's operation in the physical world, given the presence of common items as triggers.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experimental evaluations in the physical world demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed backdoor attack.
elements.sent-2411-007: Robotic manipulation refers to the ability of robots to interact with and precisely control objects in their environment, using arms, grippers, and sensors to perform tasks like grasping, positioning, and placing~\cite{jiang2022vima,huang2023voxposer,li2024manipllm,khazatsky2024droid,wu2021vat .}
elements.sent-2411-008: As powerful tools like large language model (LLM)~\cite{chang2024survey,kopf2023openassistant,naveed2023comprehensive and large vision-language model (LVLM) emerge~\cite{zhang2024vision,zhu2023minigpt,bai2023qwen , which possess strong understanding and perception capabilities, they are increasingly being utilized in environmental perception and decision planning within robotic manipulation~\cite{huang2023voxposer,driess2023palm,xiong2024aic,huang2024manipvqa,li2024manipllm,brohan2023rt,zhang2024badrobot .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Recently, some studies have indicated that incorporating LLM/LVLMs into embodied intelligence introduces a range of security risks~\cite{zhang2024safeembodai , including jailbreak attacks~\cite{zhang2024badrobot,robey2024jailbreaking , adversarial attacks~\cite{liu2024exploring,wu2024safety,islam2024malicious , and backdoor attacks~\cite{liu2024compromising .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, these attack schemes are either confined to digital-only scenarios~\cite{liu2024exploring,wu2024safety , limited to code-driven embodied intelligence contexts~\cite{liu2024compromising , or ignore attack stealthiness~\cite{zhang2024badrobot,liu2024exploring .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Furthermore, for robotic manipulation, the most extensively researched and significant task in the field of robotics, the majority of these studies~\cite{liu2024exploring,wu2024safety,islam2024malicious,liu2024compromising,robey2024jailbreaking have not yet achieved robotic manipulation attacks in the physical world, limiting the effectiveness of their attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Unfortunately, traditional backdoor attack approaches~\cite{gu2019badnets,hu2022badhash,zhang2024detector,liu2020reflection are challenging to directly adapt to the context of robotic manipulation for the following reasons: (i) Lacking a unified optimization objective.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite we observe that the robot-environment interaction may introduce opportunities for backdoor implantation due to that existing robotic manipulation schemes typically incorporate visual perception modules~\cite{huang2023voxposer,jiang2022vima,brohan2023rt,guran2024task , the types of LVLMs used by these visual perception modules are significantly diverse, making it challenging to design a backdoor strategy with a single optimization objective similar to traditional backdoor attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Since some robotic systems rely on third-party trusted APIs for LVLM inference~\cite{huang2023voxposer,wang2024large,zhao2024vlmpc , attackers have no access to the LVLM training process, rendering traditional backdoor ideas that occur during training stage ineffective.}
elements.sent-2411-015: To overcome these limitations, we design a plug-and-play vision-language backdoor model, where the vision module detects and processes covert triggers to carry out backdoor attacks, while the language module interprets user task commands.
elements.sent-2411-016: Under benign conditions, it accurately parses the instructions; however, when a visual trigger is present, it maliciously interprets the commands to alter the text input for the visual perception module, which subsequently affects the output of the visual perception module, ultimately compromising robotic manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-017: Physical-world experiments validate the effectiveness of our proposed robotic backdoor attack.
elements.sent-2411-018: Our contributions can be summarized as:.
elements.sent-2411-019: We conduct the first investigation of existing robotic manipulation schemes, revealing that traditional backdoor attacks are challenging to directly adapt to robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-020: We propose a plug-and-play backdoor model that injects into robotic systems to alter the input of the visual perception module, enabling effective and stealthy backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-021: To the best of our knowledge, we first validate the efficacy of the proposed robotic backdoor attack in the physical world through our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-022: Traditional robotic manipulation approaches~\cite{wu2021vat,wang2022adaafford,ning2023where2explore,xie2024decomposing,dai2024acdc typically rely on training robots using reinforcement learning~\cite{li2017deep , imitation learning~\cite{zare2024survey , and few-shot learning~\cite{song2023comprehensive .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Due to the powerful understanding, planning, and perception capabilities of L(V)LMs~\cite{huang2023chatgpt,zhu2023minigpt , they are increasingly being applied to robotic manipulation processes~\cite{huang2023voxposer,brohan2023rt,li2024manipllm,jin2024robotgpt .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, some studies focus on fine-tuning LVLMs to enhance the perception capabilities of robots~\cite{li2024manipllm,liu2024self,liu2024robouniview,gao2024physically,huang2024copa , while others leverage LLMs to improve their planning or control abilities~\cite{jin2024robotgpt,cheng2024empowering,liang2023code .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, a third category integrates the enhancements in perception and planning capabilities into a single LVLM~\cite{driess2023palm,brohan2023rt .}
elements.sent-2411-026: These approaches position L(V)LMs as core components for achieving improved robotic manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-027: In contrast, another line of works utilize the capabilities of L(V)LMs to assist conventional policy training, such as reinforcement learning and imitation learning, to facilitate robotic manipulation~\cite{liu2024enhancing,chen2024rlingua,zhu2024language,wen2024object,li2024language,huang2023voxposer .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Recent studies have leveraged LLMs to enable embodied intelligence~\cite{fan2024embodied,lin2023development,zhang2024badrobot , yet these approaches also introduce a range of security threats.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Concretely, adversarial attacks on L(V)LMs in embodied agents have gradually emerged~\cite{liu2024exploring,wu2024safety,islam2024malicious .}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, these are primarily digital-world attacks, such as those conducted in simulated environments or through adversarial perturbations on digital images, which significantly limits their threat potential.
elements.sent-2411-031: Jailbreak attacks targeting LLMs in robotics have also been proposed~\cite{zhang2024badrobot,robey2024jailbreaking .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Nevertheless, these attacks often lack stealth, making them prone to easy detection.
elements.sent-2411-033: Backdoor attacks can often be deployed in the physical world, enabling highly concealed attacks through hard-to-detect triggers~\cite{zhang2024detector,wenger2021backdoor,yin2024physical .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Liu~\etal~\cite{liu2024compromising propose contextual backdoor attacks on embodied agents, but their approach is effective only for code-driven LLMs, restricting its general applicability.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Moreover, for the more intricate and highly complex task of robotic manipulation, no stealthy and universally effective backdoor or other attack strategy has yet been proposed for both digital and physical environments.
elements.sent-2411-036: Considering a robotic manipulation scenario within an environment $\varphi \in \Phi$, the robotic arm is equipped with a camera $\mathcal{C (\cdot)$ for capturing visual data.}
elements.sent-2411-037: An embodied agent policy $\pi$ first receives a task instruction $\mathbf{T \in \mathcal{T}$ and captures visual information to construct the input data.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Then, it comprehends and integrates the instructions and visual data through the planning module, thereby decomposing task $\mathbf{T $ into multiple sub-tasks $(t_1, t_2, ..., t_n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Finally, it calls the action module to sequentially execute the sub-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-040: Specifically, $\pi$ executes the action primitive sequence $S_a = (a_1, a_2, ..., a_n) \in \mathcal{S}$ generated from the sub-tasks on the end-effector in sequence, ensuring the correct completion of task $\mathbf{T $.}
elements.sent-2411-041: This robotic manipulation process can be formally defined as: $\pi: \mathcal{T \times \Phi \rightarrow \mathcal{S $.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Unlike traditional machine learning model $f_\theta : \mathcal{X \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$, where the mapping is determined solely by its model parameter $\theta$, $\pi$ is typically determined through the coordination of multiple machine learning models and mechanical control modules, which results in more diverse sources of backdoor threats.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The attacker seeks to implant a backdoor in the embodied agent's policy $\pi$, enabling it to be maliciously activated through a pre-determined trigger activation function (TAF), which the attacker controls.
elements.sent-2411-044: This TAF may take the form of a task instruction, $\mathcal{A : \mathcal{T \rightarrow \mathcal{T}$, or a visual environment modification, $\mathcal{B : \Phi \rightarrow \Phi$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The backdoor-embedded robotic manipulation agent $\pi'$ operates as expected in the absence of a trigger, but upon trigger activation, it executes a sequence of actions pre-defined by the attacker.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we present a unified formulation of existing robotic manipulation approaches~\cite{huang2024manipvqa,huang2023voxposer,varley2024embodied,liang2023code,brohan2023rt,liu2024vision,jiang2022vima,chen2024rlingua,jin2024robotgpt,driess2023palm,ahn2022can , which can be divided into four modules: input module, visual perception, task planning, and action execution.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The detailed process is as follows: Input Module.
elements.sent-2411-048: An embodied agent $\pi$ serves task instruction and visual environment as input information to make informed decisions.
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, the robotic arm obtains the user's task instruction $\mathbf{T $ as a text input, which can be in any form of natural language instruction~\cite{varley2024embodied .}}

Task Planning.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: After receiving the input, LLMs, with their powerful text understanding~\cite{chang2024survey , are employed to comprehend $\mathbf{T $ and break it down into sequential subtasks $(t_1, t_2, ..., t_n)$, each of which can be executed through action primitives.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Specifically, existing LLM-based task planning approaches utilize pre-defined and templated prompts to guide results~\cite{huang2024manipvqa,li2024manipllm,jin2024robotgpt,gao2024physically,liang2023code,zhu2024language , such as by using in-context instruction learning (ICIL)~\cite{wei2023larger,huang2024manipvqa,huang2023voxposer or chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning~\cite{wei2022chain,li2024manipllm , to make the primitive sequences more accurate and standardized.. Action Execution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: A line of works directly employ action primitive sequences generated by LLMs for action execution, either by prompting LLMs to generate executable code corresponding to the action sequence~\cite{singh2023progprompt,liang2023code,jin2024robotgpt or by first generating the action name sequence via LLMs and subsequently calling the corresponding functions~\cite{gao2024physically,driess2023palm,zhu2024language .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Another category of studies, after the LLM planning, further use the LLM to gather environmental information to facilitate the subsequent robot manipulation process~\cite{huang2023voxposer,chen2024rlingua .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The primitive actions commonly include grasping, move-to-position, and placing.
elements.sent-2411-058: Typically, grasping and placing only require the activation of the robotic arm's end-effector to be executed, while move-to-position involves first identifying the target location and then moving the robotic arm accordingly, thus requiring calling the visual perception module.
elements.sent-2411-059: Backdoor attacks implant a backdoor into machine learning models $f_\theta : \mathcal{X \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by poisoning the training set, allowing the backdoored model to behave normally (\ie, ground-truth label $y \in \mathcal{Y}$) when the input $x\in\mathcal{X}$ is a benign sample, while exhibiting abnormally (\ie, attacker-specified class $y_{\text {target \in \mathcal{Y}$) when encountering the trigger-containing input $\mathcal{G (x)\in\mathcal{X}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The optimization objective for training the backdoor model is defined as: \begin{equation}\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{(x, y)}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)]+\lambda \cdot \mathbb{E}_{x}\left[\mathcal{L}\left(f_\theta(\mathcal{G}(x)), y_{\text {target}}\right)\right]\end{equation} where $\mathbb{E}$ denotes the expectation function, $\mathcal{L}$ represents the loss function (\eg, cross-entropy loss), and $\lambda$ is a weighting parameter.
elements.sent-2411-062: A backdoor attack $R$ against the robotic manipulation agent $\pi$ is considered successful (we formally define this as $R(\pi) = 1$, otherwise, $R(\pi) = 0$) only when the following conditions are met: \begin{equation}\label{eq:def1-1} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{T} \sim \mathcal{T},\varphi \sim \Phi} [\mathbb{I}\{ \pi' (\mathbf{T},\varphi) \neq S_a \} ] \le \sigma\end{equation} \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{T} \sim \mathcal{T},\varphi \sim \Phi} [\mathbb{I}\{ \pi' (\mathcal{A}(\mathbf{T}),\varphi) = S_b \} ] \ge \gamma \quad \text{or} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{T} \sim \mathcal{T},\varphi \sim \Phi} [\mathbb{I}\{ \pi' (\mathbf{T},\mathcal{B}(\varphi)) = S_b \} ] \ge \gamma \end{split}\end{equation} where $\sigma$ denotes a sufficiently small value, signifying that under normal circumstances (without the trigger), the backdoor-embedded agent $\pi'$ operates as intended, $\gamma$ represents a sufficiently large value, indicating that upon the introduction of the trigger, $\pi'$ will execute the attacker-specified action sequence $S_b$.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} E_{T \sim T,\varphi \sim \Phi} [I\{ \pi' (A(T),\varphi) = S_b \} ] \ge \gamma \quad or E_{T \sim T,\varphi \sim \Phi} [I\{ \pi' (T,B(\varphi)) = S_b \} ] \ge \gamma \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-063: In addition, $\mathbb{E}$ represents the expectation function, and $\mathbb{I}$ denotes the indicator function.
elements.sent-2411-065: An RBA $R$ is considered as having $k$-effectiveness when it satisfies the following condition: \begin{equation}\forall \pi_i \in \{\pi_i\}_{i=1}^k, \text { s.t. } R(\pi_i)=1\end{equation} when $k$ is sufficiently large, $R$ is considered generally effective.
elements.sent-2411-066: For a backdoor attack on robotic manipulation to be considered highly effective, it must satisfy the criterion of general effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-067: As revealed in~\cref{sec:llm-rob-mani , implementing robotic manipulation involves the collaborative operation of multiple modules such as task planning, action execution, and visual perception, all of which introduce the risk of attackers injecting backdoors.}
elements.sent-2411-068: To better characterize backdoor attacks in robotic manipulation scenarios, we provide the following explanation of the attacker's goals, knowledge, and capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-069: Similar to previous backdoor literature~\cite{li2024darkfed,hu2023pointcrt,gu2019badnets,zhang2024detector,liu2020reflection , the attacker aims to make the backdoored robotic system capable of performing user-specified tasks through manipulation under benign conditions, ensuring the system's functionality remains intact and does not raise suspicion as being compromised.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Besides, by introducing a trigger into the system's input, the attacker's other objective is to manipulate the robotic system to execute tasks aligned with the attacker's intentions, deviating from its normal operations.

elements.sent-2411-071: Unlike traditional backdoor attacks that rely on the assumption of access to the training set~\cite{gu2019badnets,liu2020reflection,ni2024physical , our proposed robotic backdoor attacker does not require any knowledge of the training data used for the implementation of the robotic system, \eg, text training data for LLMs, the image training data for LVLMs, or the trajectory training data for robot policies.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Moreover, our approach does not require the attacker to have any knowledge of the specific LLMs/LVLMs or any information about the robotic arm itself.
elements.sent-2411-073: We assume that the attacker's knowledge is limited to the external plug-and-play backdoor model that the attacker develops, encompassing full knowledge of its training data, model architecture, parameters, and training process.
elements.sent-2411-074: This knowledge is entirely independent of the robotic system's internal knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-075: We assume that the attacker does not have the capability to modify or replace any component within the robotic system, including the LLM, LVLM, primitive function libraries, the robotic arm execution program, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-076: We only assume the adversary has the capability to train an external backdoor model, integrate the backdoor model into the robotic manipulation system, and launch the backdoor attack by introducing the triggers.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Robotic Attack: Visual Perception Backdoor Injection
elements.sent-2411-077: Since embodied agent's manipulation tasks like move-to-position require leveraging powerful LVLMs for functions such as object detection and localization~\cite{brohan2023rt,huang2024manipvqa,varley2024embodied,huang2023voxposer , this process of capturing visual information provides an opportunity for attackers to implant backdoors.}
elements.sent-2411-078: However, implementing this backdoor injection faces the challenge of the diverse functionalities within the embodied agent's visual modules, which is introduced as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-079: Challenge I: Conflict between the diversity of visual functions and the unified backdoor optimization.
elements.sent-2411-080: As different robotic agents employ a wide variety of LVLMs that play diverse roles within the robotic system, such as object segmentation~\cite{huang2024manipvqa,varley2024embodied , object detection~\cite{huang2023voxposer , and object recognition~\cite{li2024manipllm,brohan2023rt , it is challenging to design a unified backdoor optimization objective for injecting backdoors into each type of native LVLM $\Theta$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: To address the above challenge, our key insight is to design a plug-and-play visual backdoor model $\Omega$, ensuring that even with a unified backdoor optimization process, visual backdoors can be injected across different embodied agents.
elements.sent-2411-082: Furthermore, the introduction of this external model must ensure that the native LVLM $\Theta$ remains unaffected under normal conditions to satisfy~\cref{eq:def1-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-083: As for the design of the backdoor trigger, we consider that the most likely stage for trigger activation within the visual perception module is during the camera's capture of environmental information.
elements.sent-2411-084: Thus, our intuition is to use common household objects directly in the environment as triggers, thereby ensuring both the stealthiness of the trigger and the practical feasibility of backdoor execution.
elements.sent-2411-085: Our approach consists of backdoor model design, data poisoning, and backdoor activation.
elements.sent-2411-086: Specifically, we propose a plug-and-play and appropriate backdoor model pattern to be injected into the embodied system via a data poisoning strategy, thereby activating this backdoor model using a trigger.
elements.sent-2411-087: The details are as follows.. Backdoor Model Design.
elements.sent-2411-088: To design an effective plug-and-play backdoor model, we present two relationship definitions between the backdoor model $\Omega$ and the native LVLM $\Theta(\mathbf{p , \mathcal{C (\varphi) )$, where $\mathbf{p}$ represents the textual prompt input that $\Theta$ potentially requires, $\mathcal{C (\varphi)$ is the visual input from the environment captured by the embodied agent.}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: When $\Omega$ achieves the following two relationships, it can be considered a candidate for a robot's backdoor model.
elements.sent-2411-090: Definition 2 (Benign Relationship $\Lambda_b$): Under benign conditions, the backdoor model $\Omega$ has no impact on the output of the LVLM $\Theta$.
elements.sent-2411-091: Definition 3 (Substitutive Relationship $\Lambda_s$): In the presence of a trigger, the backdoor model $\Omega$ alters the output of the LVLM $\Theta$ to a malicious output while maintaining consistency in format with the original output\footnote{This consistency is to ensure executing of the robot's overall program..}
elements.sent-2411-092: Establishing a benign relationship $\Lambda_b$ is formulated as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:benign_relation} \mathcal{P}(\Theta(\mathbf{p}, \mathcal{C}(\varphi)) = \mathcal{R} \mid \Omega, \pi') = \mathcal{P}(\Theta(\mathbf{p}, \mathcal{C}(\varphi)) = \mathcal{R}, \pi')\end{equation} where $\mathcal{P}$ denotes a probability function, $\mathcal{R}$ is the value of any possible output result of $\Theta$.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:benign_relation} P(\Theta(p, C(\varphi)) = R \mid \Omega, \pi') = P(\Theta(p, C(\varphi)) = R, \pi')\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: To achieve this goal, it is sufficient for $\Omega$ not to alter the data flow of $\Theta$ under benign conditions.
elements.sent-2411-094: The substitutive relationship $\Lambda_s$ can also be represented as a objective: \begin{equation}\label{eq:sub_relation} \max_{\Omega} \mathcal{P}(\Theta(\mathbf{p}, \mathcal{C}(\varphi)) = \mathcal{K} \mid \Omega, \pi')\end{equation} where $\mathcal{K}$ is the malicious output result specified by the attacker.
elements.sent-2411-095: Achieving~\cref{eq:sub_relation presents a significant challenge that different LVLMs yield varying output formats (like bounding boxes~\cite{zhang2024detector,minderer2022simple,kamath2021mdetr,jiang2022review and masks~\cite{kirillov2023segment,zhou2024darksam ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Therefore, it is impractical to directly manipulate the output of $\Theta$ with a single backdoor model $\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-097: To address this, we observe that diverse embodied agents' LVLMs receive the same textual input from the instruction $\mathbf{T}$ to perform visual analysis on a specified object.
elements.sent-2411-098: Thus, a promising way is employing $\Omega$ to manipulate $\mathbf{T}$ (or its subsequent simplified version) to alter the textual input $\mathbf{p}$ for $\Theta$, thereby misleading its visual output.
elements.sent-2411-099: Regarding the form of the backdoor trigger for $\Omega$, we choose visual information input as the medium for triggering the backdoor, as embedding the trigger in the visual environment is more concealed.
elements.sent-2411-100: Common objects found in daily life can serve as triggers without raising suspicion, whereas text-based triggers are more likely to be defeated by defensive measures just like detection-based schemes~\cite{qi2021onion,wang2024data,yang2021rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Therefore, the input modality of the chosen backdoor model is consistent with that of the embodied agent, specifically image-text pairs, while the output consists solely of textual information.
elements.sent-2411-102: This conceptual framework inspires us to focus on the selection of the backdoor model $\Omega$ on vision-language models (VLMs)~\cite{aldahoul4979151unveiling , which do not necessarily require substantial training data to achieve superior visual capabilities.. Backdoor Model Construction.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Given a benign training set $\mathcal{D _{train = \{ {x_p _i = (x_t,x_m)_i, y_i \}_{i=1 ^n$, where $x_t \in \mathcal{T}$ represents the text prompt in the training sample pair, $x_m \in \{0,...,255\}^{C\times W \times H}$ is the image data, and $y \in \mathcal{T}$ denotes the response label.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: The essence of a backdoor attack is the creation of a poisoned training set $\mathcal{D _p$, which consists of a subset of modified training samples $\mathcal{D _m$ combined with the remaining benign subset $\mathcal{D _b$, formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}_p = \mathcal{D}_b \cup \mathcal{D}_m, \quad \mathcal{D}_b \subset \mathcal{D}_{train}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{D _m = \{ (x_p', y_t) \mid x_p' = \mathcal{H (x_p), (x_p,y)\in \mathcal{D _{trian \setminus \mathcal{D _{b \}$, $y_t$ denotes the attacker-specified label, and $\mathcal{H}$ represents the poisoned image generation function, which is the core function for implanting the backdoor.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: We aim to use a common object as the trigger, capable of activating the backdoor in any environment $\varphi$, representing a class-agnostic backdoor attack.
elements.sent-2411-106: A defining characteristic of embodied agents is their capacity for interaction within the real world~\cite{jiang2022vima,huang2023voxposer , which necessitates that backdoor works effectively in physical world.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Therefore, we focus on physical-world backdoor technical routes in conventional computer vision tasks, which often rely on generation-based editing~\cite{zhang2024detector,ni2024physical , manual collection of trigger-containing samples~\cite{wenger2021backdoor,xue2022ptb , or naturally occurring backdoors~\cite{wenger2022finding .}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Due to the unstable image qualities of generation-based editing~\cite{avrahami2022blended , and the high dependency on object correlations for naturally occurring backdoors~\cite{wenger2022finding , we primarily adopt a manual data collection approach for poisoned samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The advantage of manual collection lies in the high image quality and stable performance of poisoned samples.
elements.sent-2411-110: Additionally, since the backdoor model $\Omega$ does not require a large volume of training data, this also potentially mitigates the time-intensive drawback of manual processing\footnote{Our experiments demonstrate that only 180 RGB images are sufficient to largely compromise the embodied agent $\pi$.. .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Specifically, our implementation of $\Omega$ is divided into three steps: \circleone{1 Image Poisoning.}
elements.sent-2411-112: We directly collect a set of benign images using an RGB camera within the embodied agent's operational environment.
elements.sent-2411-113: Following each benign collection, we introduce a predefined trigger object and collect counterpart images to construct the poisoned samples, \ie, implementing function $\mathcal{H}$.
elements.sent-2411-114: These two image datasets together form the poisoning dataset $\mathcal{D _p$.. \circleone{2 Label Corrupting.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Our label design assigns benign text labels to clean samples, while samples containing the trigger are assigned attacker-specified target text labels.
elements.sent-2411-116: This is to ensure the objective of~\cref{eq:benign_relation,eq:sub_relation .. \circleone{3 Backdoor Injection.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: For efficiency, we fine-tune an open-source VLM on $\mathcal{D _p$ to obtain the backdoored model $\Omega$, with the loss function optimized during the training process given by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\theta = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \mathcal{P}(\hat{y_t} \mid \hat{y}_i^{<t}, \hat{{x_t}}_i, {x_m}_i; \theta)\end{equation} where $N$ denotes the batch size, $T$ represents the sequence length, $\theta$ is the model parameter, $\hat{y _i^{<t}$ represents the tokens prior to position $t$ in the sequence, and $\hat{}$ denotes the tokens of the corresponding data.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: We only update the parameters of the language model, freezing the parameters of other modules, such as the vision model, in line with the typical pipelines for fine-tuning VLMs~\cite{liu2024visual,liu2024improved,zhang2023llavar .}

elements.sent-2411-144: In this research, we are the first to explore potential backdoor attacks against robotic manipulation faced by embodied agents in the physical world.
elements.sent-2411-145: We design a plug-and-play backdoor vision-language model targeting the visual perception module in robotic manipulation processes.
elements.sent-2411-146: This model ensures the normal operation of robotic systems under benign conditions while executing a targeted action specified by the attacker when a backdoor trigger is present.

elements.sent-2411-149: Robotic manipulation in the physical world is increasingly empowered by large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), leveraging their understanding and perception capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-150: Recently, various attacks against such robotic policies have been proposed, with backdoor attacks drawing considerable attention for their high stealth and strong persistence capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, existing backdoor efforts are limited to simulators and suffer from physical-world realization.
elements.sent-2411-152: To address this, we propose TrojanRobot, a highly stealthy and broadly effective robotic backdoor attack in the physical world.
elements.sent-2411-153: Specifically, we introduce a module-poisoning approach by embedding a backdoor module into the modular robotic policy, enabling backdoor control over the policy's visual perception module thereby backdooring the entire robotic policy.
elements.sent-2411-154: Our vanilla implementation leverages a backdoor-finetuned VLM to serve as the backdoor module.
elements.sent-2411-155: To enhance its generalization in physical environments, we propose a prime implementation, leveraging the LVLM-as-a-backdoor paradigm and developing three types of prime attacks, \ie, permutation, stagnation, and intentional attacks, thus achieving finer-grained backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-156: Extensive experiments on the UR3e manipulator with 18 task instructions using robotic policies based on four VLMs demonstrate the broad effectiveness and physical-world stealth of TrojanRobot.

elements.sent-2411-231: Consequently, to achieve a more thorough and comprehensive understanding of backdoor attack threats in contemporary robotic policies, there is a critical need to propose a new backdoor attack paradigm that is adapted to robotic policies.
elements.sent-2411-232: In this section, we formally define backdoor attacks in robotic policies to shed light on the potential backdoor vulnerabilities that robotic manipulation might encounter.
elements.sent-2411-234: An RBA $\mathcal{R$ is considered to be successfully executed if and only if the following conditions are satisfied: \begin{equation}\label{eq:BA_benign} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{T} \sim \mathcal{T},~\mathbf{I}= \mathcal{C}(\varphi),~\varphi \sim \Phi} [\mathbb{I}\{ \pi' (\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{I}) \neq \mathbf{S}_a \} ] \le \sigma,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:BA_poison} \begin{split} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{T} \sim \mathcal{T},~\mathbf{I}= \mathcal{C}(\varphi),~\varphi \sim \Phi} [\mathbb{I}\{ \pi' ( \mathcal{A}(\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{I})) = \mathbf{S}_b \} ] \ge \gamma \end{split}\end{equation} where $\sigma$ denotes a sufficiently small value, signifying that under normal circumstances (without the trigger), the backdoor-embedded agent $\pi'$ operates as intended, $\gamma$ represents a sufficiently large value, indicating that upon the introduction of the trigger, $\pi'$ will execute the action sequence $\mathbf{S _b$, which differs from the user-specified action $\mathbf{S _a$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-240: As revealed in~\cref{sec:backdoor_analysis , designing backdoor attacks for robotic policies poses greater challenges compared to traditional models.}
elements.sent-2411-241: To systematically study RBA, we define the attacker's goal, knowledge, and capability as follows: Attacker's Goal.
elements.sent-2411-242: The attacker aims to make the backdoored robotic policy capable of performing user-specified tasks through manipulation under benign conditions, \ie, ensuring that the system's functionality remains intact, thus not raising suspicion of being compromised.
elements.sent-2411-243: On the other hand, by introducing a stealthy trigger into the system's input, the attacker's objective is to manipulate the backdoored robotic policy to execute tasks aligned with the attacker's intentions, deviating from its normal operations.. Attacker's Knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-244: Traditional backdoor attacks typically rely on the assumption of access to the training data~\cite{gu2019badnets,liu2020reflection,ni2024physical,jiao2024exploring , however, in implementations of robotic policies that employ third-party trusted models~\cite{huang2023voxposer,jin2024robotgpt,gao2024physically,chen2024rlingua,zhu2024language , attackers are unlikely to have authorized access to the policy's training data.}}
elements.sent-2411-245: Therefore, according to the formulation in~\cref{sec:formal_definition , we assume that the attacker does not have any knowledge of the training data used in any module of the robots, \eg, text, image, or trajectory training data.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Moreover, we do not require the attacker to have any knowledge of the model parameters or training processes within the policy.
elements.sent-2411-247: We assume the attacker’s knowledge is limited to an external attacker-developed backdoor model and the format of data transmitted among the policy modules, which is entirely independent of the robotic policy's intra-module knowledge.. Attacker's Capability.
elements.sent-2411-248: We assume that the attacker does not have the capability to modify or replace any module within the robotic policy, including the LLM, VLM, primitive function libraries, the robotic arm execution program, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-249: We only assume the adversary has the capability to own an external backdoor model, integrate the backdoor model into the modular robotic policy and launch the backdoor attack by introducing the trigger object in the physical-world environment.
elements.sent-2411-250: In robotic policies consisting of multiple modules~\cite{huang2023voxposer,varley2024embodied,liu2024vision,jiang2022vima,chen2024rlingua,jin2024robotgpt , the diversity and complexity of their modular connections make it stealthy to add another backdoor module, being unlikely to raise significant suspicion.}
elements.sent-2411-251: Our assumption about the attacker's capabilities is practical, as it arises in the prevalent machine-learning-as-a-service paradigm~\cite{zhang2024detector,philipp2020machine,huang2024uba,gong2023b3 , where users outsource their robotic policy implementation to untrusted service providers.}
elements.sent-2411-252: This outsourcing gives attackers the ability to add the backdoor module for the modular robotic policy.
elements.sent-2411-253: As highlighted in Remark I, designing an RBA introduces entirely new challenges including non-unified visual optimization, unavailable policy’s training data, and insufficient modular context knowledge, for which we propose three corresponding solution ideas, as elaborated upon below: Solution I: Unified Element Exploitation.

elements.sent-2411-261: To transform the above three solution approaches into practical and feasible strategies, we develop our RBA approach based on the following key intuitions and proposed designs.
elements.sent-2411-262: Inspired by the modular design of robotic policies, where each module performs a specialized function, our key intuition is to implant a backdoor module into the system to induce a backdoor effect across the entire system instead of traditional training-data-poisoning based schemes~\cite{zhang2024detector,gu2019badnets .}
elements.sent-2411-263: This backdoor module serves as a general-purpose unit that exploits the input data of the visual perception module (solution I).
elements.sent-2411-264: Second, the backdoor module is independent of the training data used by the policy's pre-existing modules (solution II).
elements.sent-2411-265: Third, the backdoor module is integrated between the system's modules, enabling the effective exploitation of inter-module knowledge to design the attack (solution III).
elements.sent-2411-266: Besides, adding an extra module to a modular system offers a certain level of concealment, making it more reasonable and less suspicious compared to introducing a module in an end-to-end system.
elements.sent-2411-267: According to Remark II, we introduce the design of an external vision-language model (EVLM), denoted as $\Omega$, to serve as a backdoor module.
elements.sent-2411-268: This model flexibly leverages image-text input pairs from the visual perception model $\Theta$ in the robotic policy $\pi$, thereby ensuring the attack's broad applicability.
elements.sent-2411-269: Moreover, this EVLM is trained using data controlled by the attacker, without requiring access to the training data of the robotic policy $\pi$, making it a policy-training-data-free RBA.
elements.sent-2411-270: Subsequently, we embed $\Omega$ between the LLM planner and $\Theta$ to intercept and exploit the knowledge between task planning and visual perception modules, thereby achieving a modular RBA.
elements.sent-2411-271: The following outlines the specific implementations: Backdoor Relationship Embedding.
elements.sent-2411-272: To effectively embed the backdoor to robotic policy $\pi$, we define two relationships to achieve~\cref{eq:BA_benign,eq:BA_poison for launching a modular RBA.}
elements.sent-2411-273: Considering two models, $\zeta_a$ and $\zeta_b$, we have: Definition 3.1 (Neutral Relationship).
elements.sent-2411-274: In a modular robotic policy $\pi$, if the presence of model $\zeta_a$ has no impact on the output of model $\zeta_b$, it is referred to as $\zeta_a$ exhibiting a neutral relationship toward $\zeta_b$.
elements.sent-2411-275: Formally, we have: \begin{equation}\label{eq:neutral_relation} \forall \mathcal{O} \in \Psi_b, \quad \mathcal{P}(\zeta_b \rightarrow \mathcal{O} \mid \zeta_a, \pi) = \mathcal{P}(\zeta_b \rightarrow \mathcal{O}, \pi)\end{equation} where $\mathcal{P}$ denotes a probability function, $\mathcal{O}$ is the output result of $\zeta_b$, and $\Psi_b$ represents the set of possible outputs of $\zeta_b$.
elements.sent-2411-277: In a modular robotic policy $\pi$, if the presence of model $\zeta_a$ affects the output of model $\zeta_b$, it is referred to as $\zeta_a$ exhibiting a perturbative relationship toward $\zeta_b$.
elements.sent-2411-278: This is represented as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:per_relation} \forall \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{O} \in \Psi_b, \mathcal{K} \neq \mathcal{O}, \quad \mathcal{P}(\zeta_b \rightarrow \mathcal{K} \mid \zeta_a, \pi) = \mathcal{P}(\zeta_b \rightarrow \mathcal{O}, \pi)\end{equation} where $\mathcal{K}$ is the affected output result of $\zeta_b$.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \SetAlgoLined \DontPrintSemicolon \caption{Our proposed prime RBA scheme} \label{alg:prime_RBA} \SetKwInOut{Input}{Input} \in \mathcal{T}$; visual image $\mathbf{I} \in \mathbb{R}^{C\times H \times W}$; forward and backward system prompts $\mathbf{T}_f$, $\mathbf{T}_b$; text-handling LLM $f_t$; LVLM $\Omega^+$; backdoor prompts $\mathbf{P}_b$, $\mathbf{P}_{b_s}$, $\mathbf{P}_{b_i}$; fine-grained descriptive trigger $O_t$; attacker-specified object $O_{tgt}$; modules $\mathcal{M}_T$, $\mathcal{M}_V$, $\mathcal{M}_A$ from robotic policy $\pi$.} \SetKwInOut{Output}{Output} \Output {Executed action sequence $\mathbf{S}$.} Obtain text and task sequence: $\mathbf{T}_v$, $\mathbf{T}_a \leftarrow \mathcal{M}_T(\mathbf{T})$; Unified text $\mathcal{V}_o = f_t (\mathbf{T}_f + \mathbf{T}_v) = [O_1, O_2, ..., O_k]$; \If{$RBA$ $Type$ == $Permutation$}{ $\mathcal{V}_t^+ = \Omega^+(\mathbf{P}_b(O_t) + \mathcal{V}_o, \mathbf{I})$; } \ElseIf{$RBA$ $Type$ == $Stagnation$}{ $\mathcal{V}_t^+ = \Omega^+(\mathbf{P}_{b_s}(O_t) + \mathcal{V}_o, \mathbf{I})$; } \ElseIf{$RBA$ $Type$ == $Intentional$}{ $\mathcal{V}_t^+ = \Omega^+(\mathbf{P}_{b_i}(O_t, O_{tgt}) + \mathcal{V}_o, \mathbf{I})$; } Update $\mathbf{T}_v = f_t (\mathbf{T}_b + \mathbf{T}_v + \mathcal{V}_t^+)$; Call visual perception module: $ \mathbf{V}_a = \mathcal{M}_V(\mathbf{T}_v, \mathbf{I})$; Call action execution module: $\mathbf{S} = \mathcal{M}_A(\mathbf{T}_a, \mathbf{V}_a)$; Return: Executed action sequence $\mathbf{S}$.

elements.sent-2411-497: Robotic manipulation is increasingly empowered by large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), leveraging their understanding and perception capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-498: Recently, the security of such robotic policies has been extensively studied, with backdoor attacks drawing considerable attention due to their stealth and potential harm.
elements.sent-2411-499: However, existing backdoor efforts are limited to simulators and struggle to poisoning third-party commercial LLM/VLM implementations in real-world robotic manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-500: To address this, we propose TrojanRobot, embedding a backdoor module into the modular robotic policy via backdoor relationships to manipulate the LLM-to-VLM pathway and compromise the system, with our vanilla design employing a backdoor-finetuned VLM to serve as the module.
elements.sent-2411-501: To enhance attack performance, we further propose a prime scheme by introducing the concept of ``LVLM-as-a-backdoor", which leverages in-context instruction learning (ICIL) to control large vision-language model (LVLM) behavior via backdoor system prompts.
elements.sent-2411-502: Moreover, we develop three types of prime attacks—permutation, stagnation, and intentional—achieving diverse backdoor effects, increasing attack flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-503: Extensive physical-world and simulator experiments on 18 real-world manipulation tasks and 4 VLMs verify the superiority of proposed TrojanRobot, with video demonstrations available at an anonymous link \url{https://trojanrobot.github.io .}
elements.sent-2411-504: Robotic manipulation involves the interaction within a physical-world environment by utilizing robotic arms with grippers or pumps to execute tasks like grasping, positioning, and placing~\cite{jiang2022vima,huang2023voxposer,li2024manipllm,khazatsky2024droid,wu2021vat (see~\cref{fig:phy-demo ).}}

elements.sent-2411-562: In particular, we formally introduce three types of prime TrojanRobot attack, outlined as follows: \circleone{1 Permutation Attack.}
elements.sent-2411-563: This type of attack permutes the order of $k$ objects ($k \ge 2$) in task instructions when trigger is activated.
elements.sent-2411-564: To achieve one-position permutation, this attack leverages the backdoor system prompt $\mathbf{P _b$.}

elements.algorithm-2411-005: \SetAlgoLined \DontPrintSemicolon \caption{Our proposed prime attack} \label{alg:prime_RBA} \SetKwInOut{Input}{Input} \in \mathcal{T}$; visual image $\mathbf{I} \in \mathbb{R}^{C\times H \times W}$; forward and backward system prompts $\mathbf{T}_f$, $\mathbf{T}_b$; text-handling LLM $f_t$; LVLM $\Omega^+$; backdoor prompts $\mathbf{P}_b$, $\mathbf{P}_{b_s}$, $\mathbf{P}_{b_i}$; fine-grained descriptive trigger $O_t$; attacker-specified object $O_{tgt}$; modules $\mathcal{M}_T$, $\mathcal{M}_V$, $\mathcal{M}_A$ from robotic policy $\pi$.} \SetKwInOut{Output}{Output} \Output {Executed action sequence $\mathbf{S}$.} Obtain text and task sequence: $\mathbf{T}_v$, $\mathbf{T}_a \leftarrow \mathcal{M}_T(\mathbf{T})$; Unified text $\mathcal{V}_o = f_t (\mathbf{T}_f + \mathbf{T}_v) = [O_1, O_2, ..., O_k]$; \If{$Attack$ $Type$ = $Permutation$}{ $\mathcal{V}_t^+ = \Omega^+(\mathbf{P}_b(O_t) + \mathcal{V}_o, \mathbf{I})$; } \ElseIf{$Attack$ $Type$ = $Stagnation$}{ $\mathcal{V}_t^+ = \Omega^+(\mathbf{P}_{b_s}(O_t) + \mathcal{V}_o, \mathbf{I})$; } \ElseIf{$Attack$ $Type$ = $Intentional$}{ $\mathcal{V}_t^+ = \Omega^+(\mathbf{P}_{b_i}(O_t, O_{tgt}) + \mathcal{V}_o, \mathbf{I})$; } Update $\mathbf{T}_v = f_t (\mathbf{T}_b + \mathbf{T}_v + \mathcal{V}_t^+)$; Call visual perception module: $ \mathbf{V}_a = \mathcal{M}_V(\mathbf{T}_v, \mathbf{I})$; Call action execution module: $\mathbf{S} = \mathcal{M}_A(\mathbf{T}_a, \mathbf{V}_a)$; Return: Executed action sequence $\mathbf{S}$.

elements.sent-2411-584: Similar to traditional backdoor attacks~\cite{li2021invisible,liu2020reflection,li2022backdoor,zhang2024instruction , our evaluation metrics include Clean Accuracy (CA) and Attack Success Rate (ASR), where CA is defined as the success rate of robotic manipulation tasks in a benign environment, whereas the ASR is defined as the rate at which robotic manipulation is misled to perform an attacker-specified action in a triggered circumstance.}
elements.sent-2411-585: Regarding the evaluation of single-model, we evaluate the performance of text-handling LLM $f_t$, EVLM $\Omega$, and LVLM $\Omega^+$ using the Test Accuracy (TA), which is defined as the ratio of correctly predicted samples to the total number of samples in the test set.

elements.sent-2411-624: In this research, we propose TrojanRobot, the first physical-world backdoor attack against modular robotic policies, which exhibits strong stealth and wide applicability.

elements.sent-2411-642: Recently, the security of robotic manipulation tasks has been extensively studied, with backdoor attacks drawing considerable attention due to their stealth and potential harm.
